title: Plectic Lie algebra action on the cohomology of Hilbert modular varieties
speaker: Lue Pan
abstract:
A result of Jan Nekovář says that the Galois action on p-adic intersection cohomology of Hilbert modular varieties with coefficients in automorphic local systems is semisimple. We will explain a new proof of this result for the non-CM part of the cohomology and extend it to the locally analytic completed cohomology. Interestingly, Nekovář’s approach is based on the construction of partial Frobenii at places away from p, while our method uses partial Sen operators at p to construct a plectic Lie algebra action (whose meaning will be explained in the talk). 
This is joint work in progress with Yuanyang Jiang.

proof_stages.label: General semisimplicity problem and modular-curve benchmark
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from the general question whether the Galois action on the cohomology of automorphic local systems is already semisimple, and recalls the modular-curve case where Hecke decomposition plus the Eichler-Shimura/Galois-representation relation gives semisimplicity.

proof_stages.label: Hilbert modular reduction to tensor induction and its obstruction
proof_stages.summary: For a totally real field F, Hilbert modular varieties and their intersection cohomology are set up, and the standard Hecke-trace argument identifies the semisimplification of a Hecke eigenspace with copies of the tensor induction of a two-dimensional G_F-representation, whose reducibility as a G_Q-representation makes extension classes the real problem.

proof_stages.label: Strongly irreducible eigenpackets and completed-cohomology theorem
proof_stages.summary: After defining strong irreducibility, the main result is stated: under strong irreducibility and a mild non-scalar p-adic inertia condition, the relevant Hecke-isotypic part of usual/intersection cohomology and, more strongly, finite-dimensional subrepresentations of middle-degree completed cohomology are built from copies of the full tensor induction.

proof_stages.label: Plectic hidden-symmetry heuristic
proof_stages.summary: The guiding explanation is the Nekovář-Scholl plectic symmetry conjecture: tensor induction is irreducible for the larger plectic group, so a plectic or local plectic action would force the full tensor-induced representation and hence semisimplicity after restriction to G_Q.

proof_stages.label: Plectic Lie algebra action on locally analytic completed cohomology
proof_stages.summary: The technical theorem constructs, on the locally analytic part of completed cohomology localized at a suitable Hecke eigensystem, an action of the product of the Lie algebras of the p-adic images of the conjugate two-dimensional Galois representations, extending the ordinary G_Q-Lie algebra action and forcing the complete tensor-induction isotypic structure.

proof_stages.label: Partial Sen operators as geometric generators
proof_stages.summary: For each p-adic embedding of F, a commuting partial Sen operator is constructed geometrically from the infinite-level Hilbert modular variety, and the intended plectic Lie algebra is recovered by taking the smallest Galois-stable Lie subalgebras generated by these operators, in the spirit of Sen’s theorem.

proof_stages.label: Control by deformation-theoretic bounds and dense classical points
proof_stages.summary: The generated Lie algebras are shown to have the correct size and commutation properties by bounding them via local pseudo-deformation rings and verifying the desired identities on a dense non-CM classical subspace with distinct Hodge-Tate weights, where strong irreducibility makes the tensor induction irreducible.

examples.name: Modular-curve semisimplicity benchmark
examples.note: The modular curve with symmetric-power local systems is used to display the familiar Hecke-eigenspace argument, where Eichler-Shimura identifies each eigenspace with copies of an irreducible two-dimensional Galois representation, thereby contrasting with the Hilbert modular case.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker works through the modular-curve case by setting up the local system, decomposing by Hecke eigenvalues, attaching rho_lambda, and using irreducibility/Eichler-Shimura to prove semisimplicity

examples.name: Distinct Hodge-Tate weights satisfying the non-scalar condition
examples.note: A Galois representation with distinct Hodge-Tate weights at one p-adic embedding is given as an easy situation where the inertia Lie algebra is not scalar, clarifying the technical hypothesis and the excluded parallel-weight-one case.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker checks the technical non-scalar hypothesis by observing that distinct Hodge-Tate weights in one embedding force the relevant inertia/Sen operator not to be scalar

examples.name: Partial complex conjugations on upper-half-plane factors
examples.note: The complex uniformization of a Hilbert modular variety by a product of upper half-planes is used as an Archimedean analogy for having one local operator on each factor, making the idea of partial Sen operators more concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: partial complex conjugation on upper-half-plane factors is invoked as an easier complex analogue of partial Sen operators, with only a brief description and no construction or calculation carried out
